items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Imagine you're convinced a certain restaurant has the best food in town. You go back again and again, and every good meal just proves you were right. But what about the times the food was mediocre? Chances are, you barely noticed. This tendency has a name in psychology: confirmation bias. Confirmation bias is our habit of searching for, favoring, and remembering information that supports what we already believe, while quietly ignoring evidence that contradicts it. It's not that we're being dishonest. Our brains just find it easier to accept ideas that fit what we already think. A classic study illustrates this nicely. Researchers gave participants a series of cards and asked them to figure out a hidden rule for arranging numbers. Most people tested only sequences they expected to be correct. Very few tried sequences designed to prove themselves wrong, even though that's exactly the strategy that would reveal the real rule fastest. So people kept confirming their guess instead of challenging it. Now, why does this matter? Confirmation bias shapes far more than restaurant choices. It affects how juries weigh evidence, how doctors form diagnoses, and how we evaluate news that agrees or disagrees with our politics. Once we form an opinion, we tend to collect support for it rather than test it. The takeaway is that good decision-making often requires the opposite instinct. Instead of asking, what proves me right, the smarter question is, what would prove me wrong? Deliberately seeking out disconfirming evidence is uncomfortable, but it's one of the most reliable ways to think more clearly.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A mental tendency to favor information that confirms existing beliefs
options.B: Why people prefer certain restaurants over others in a town
options.C: How juries are selected and instructed before a criminal trial
options.D: The process by which doctors are trained to diagnose patients

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 概括了整篇讲座的核心——确认偏误，即倾向于支持已有信念的信息。B 只是开头的引子例子，too_narrow，并非主旨。C 提到了陪审团，但那只是结尾的一个应用举例，属于 wrong_topic。D 借用了医生这一词，但讲座并非讲诊断训练，是 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is confirmation bias?

options.A: A deliberate effort to deceive others about one's true opinions
options.B: A habit of favoring information that supports existing beliefs
options.C: A method scientists use to design controlled experiments
options.D: A skill that improves the speed of complex decision-making

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 与教授给出的定义一致：偏好支持已有信念的信息。A 错在教授明确说这并非不诚实（not dishonest），属 wrong_detail。C 与科学方法相关但讲座未如此定义，是 topic_adjacent。D 把它说成提升决策速度，恰恰相反，确认偏误妨碍清晰思考，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What did most participants do in the card study?

options.A: They refused to guess any rule for arranging the numbers
options.B: They quickly discovered the hidden rule by testing odd cases
options.C: They tested only sequences they expected to be correct
options.D: They worked together to compare several competing rules

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 直接对应讲座：大多数人只测试他们预期正确的序列。A 与事实相反，参与者确实在猜规则，属 wrong_detail。B 说他们很快发现规则，但讲座说很少有人用能最快揭示规则的策略，属 reversed_logic。D 提到合作比较，讲座未提及，是 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about good decision-making?

options.A: It depends mainly on collecting as much supporting evidence as possible
options.B: It is impossible once a person has formed any opinion at all
options.C: It matters only in professional fields like medicine and law
options.D: It often requires actively looking for evidence against one's view

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与结尾观点一致：好的决策需要主动寻找反驳自己的证据。A 与教授建议相反，他批评只收集支持证据，属 reversed_logic。B 把困难夸大为不可能，属 over_generalization。C 把范围窄化为专业领域，但教授说它影响日常选择，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how urban heat islands affect local ecosystems
items.transcript: Walk from a leafy park into a dense downtown on a summer afternoon, and you can feel the temperature jump. That difference is the signature of an urban heat island. An urban heat island is a metropolitan area that stays significantly warmer than the surrounding countryside, often by several degrees, especially at night. The cause is largely physical. Dark asphalt and concrete absorb solar radiation during the day and release it slowly after sunset, while sparse vegetation means little cooling from evapotranspiration. But what interests ecologists is how this altered climate reshapes the living community. Consider plants first. In many cities, trees leaf out earlier in spring and drop their leaves later in fall, extending the growing season. That sounds beneficial, but it can desynchronize plants from the insects and birds that depend on them. A caterpillar that hatches on a schedule tuned to cooler temperatures may emerge after the leaves it eats have already toughened. Insects respond too. Warmer nights let certain pests survive winters they once couldn't, and some species reproduce additional generations per year. One study of scale insects found they were far more abundant on city trees than on identical trees in cooler rural sites, and the warmth, not pollution, was the key driver. These shifts ripple upward. Birds that time their nesting to peak caterpillar abundance may find the peak has moved or shrunk. So the urban heat island is not merely an engineering nuisance about air conditioning bills. It is quietly editing the timing and composition of urban ecosystems, creating novel communities that don't exactly match anything in the surrounding region.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How cities can lower their summer air conditioning costs
options.B: How elevated urban temperatures reshape local ecological communities
options.C: Why asphalt and concrete absorb more sunlight than soil does
options.D: How pollution rather than warmth drives pest outbreaks in cities

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 抓住主旨：城市升温如何重塑当地生态群落。A 是结尾否定的内容（不仅仅是空调账单问题），属 wrong_topic。C 只是解释成因的一个细节，too_narrow。D 与讲座相反，研究表明是温度而非污染在起作用，属 surface_word_trap/reversed。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do downtown areas stay warmer at night?

options.A: Tall buildings block cooling winds from the surrounding countryside
options.B: City air contains more pollutants that trap rising heat
options.C: Dark pavement absorbs daytime heat and releases it slowly
options.D: Urban vegetation produces extra warmth through respiration

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与讲座一致：深色路面白天吸热、夜间缓慢释放。A 提到建筑挡风，讲座未说，是 topic_adjacent。B 把原因归于污染，但教授强调是物理热量，属 wrong_detail。D 说植被产生热量，与讲座所述植被通过蒸腾降温相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What did the study of scale insects find?

options.A: Scale insects disappeared from trees once cities grew warmer
options.B: Pollution was the main reason the insects became more common
options.C: Rural trees hosted more insects than identical city trees
options.D: City trees carried far more insects than cooler rural trees

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与讲座一致：城市树木上的介壳虫远多于较凉爽的乡村树木。A 与事实相反，虫子更多而非消失，属 reversed_logic。B 把原因归为污染，但讲座说温度才是关键，属 wrong_detail。C 把城乡数量关系颠倒，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about urban ecosystems?

options.A: They can develop into communities unlike those of the surrounding region
options.B: They will eventually become identical to nearby rural ecosystems
options.C: They are harmed only by the rising cost of cooling buildings
options.D: They support fewer total insect species than rural areas do

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与结尾呼应：城市生态形成与周边地区不完全匹配的新型群落。B 与之相反，讲座说它们不匹配周边，属 reversed_logic。C 把问题局限于空调成本，教授明确否定，属 topic_adjacent。D 说城市昆虫种类更少，讲座未支持且与害虫增多的细节冲突，属 over_generalization。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how green roofs reduce urban stormwater runoff
items.transcript: When a heavy rain hits a city, the water has almost nowhere to go. It sheets off rooftops and pavement, rushes into storm drains all at once, and can overwhelm the system in minutes. One increasingly popular solution sits right above our heads: the green roof. A green roof is a building roof partly or fully covered with vegetation planted over a waterproof membrane and a layer of growing medium. Its central job, from a stormwater perspective, is to slow water down. Here's how it works. When rain falls on a conventional roof, nearly all of it runs off immediately. On a green roof, the soil and plants act like a sponge. They absorb a substantial portion of the rainfall, and much of what they hold is later returned to the atmosphere through evaporation and plant transpiration. The water that does drain off leaves more gradually, spread out over hours rather than arriving in one sudden surge. Studies in several cities have measured this directly. A typical green roof can retain roughly half of annual rainfall, and during smaller storms it may capture nearly all of it. That matters because it's the sudden peak flow, not the total volume, that floods streets and triggers sewage overflows. By flattening that peak, green roofs ease pressure on aging drainage infrastructure. And the benefits stack up. The same vegetation that manages water also cools the building, filters pollutants from the runoff, and provides habitat for insects and birds. So a green roof isn't just a garden in the sky. It's a piece of working infrastructure quietly doing a job the storm drains can't.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How storm drains are designed and maintained in older cities
options.B: Why rooftop gardens have become a popular landscaping trend
options.C: How green roofs help manage stormwater in urban areas
options.D: The kinds of plants best suited for growing on city rooftops

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 概括主旨：绿色屋顶如何帮助管理城市雨水。A 谈下水道的设计维护，是 wrong_topic。B 把它当作景观潮流，忽略了核心的雨水功能，属 surface_word_trap。D 讲适合的植物种类，是 too_narrow 的细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how does a green roof handle rainfall?

options.A: It channels all rainwater directly into the storm drains below
options.B: It heats the water so that it evaporates almost instantly
options.C: It seals the rain beneath a membrane to prevent any runoff
options.D: Its soil and plants absorb water and release it gradually

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与讲座一致：土壤和植物吸水并逐渐释放。A 说直接排入下水道，与绿色屋顶减缓径流的作用相反，属 reversed_logic。B 说加热使其立即蒸发，讲座未提加热，是 wrong_detail。C 说完全封住不排水，与讲座说水会逐渐排出相矛盾，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What did studies in several cities measure about green roofs?

options.A: A typical green roof can retain about half of annual rainfall
options.B: Green roofs increase the total volume of water reaching drains
options.C: Green roofs cost more to maintain than conventional flat roofs
options.D: Most green roofs fail to capture water during smaller storms

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 直接对应讲座给出的数据：典型绿色屋顶可保留约一半的年降雨量。B 与事实相反，绿色屋顶减少径流量，属 reversed_logic。C 谈维护成本，讲座未提及，是 topic_adjacent。D 说小雨时几乎留不住水，恰与讲座所述（小雨时几乎全部留住）相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say it is the peak flow, not the total volume, that causes flooding?

options.A: To suggest that total annual rainfall has been steadily increasing
options.B: To explain why slowing the water's arrival is what truly helps
options.C: To restate that heavy rain rushes into storm drains all at once
options.D: To prove that storm drains should be replaced entirely with roofs

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 解释了教授提到峰值流量的目的：说明为何减缓水的到达（削峰）才真正有用。A 谈年降雨量增加，与该句目的无关，属 wrong_purpose。C 只是复述讲座中的现象，没解释为什么提它，属 literal_restatement。D 说应完全用屋顶取代下水道，是教授未表达的过度推断，属 over_inference。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how the camera obscura influenced Renaissance painting techniques
items.transcript: Look at certain paintings from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, and you may notice something striking: the perspective is almost photographically precise, the play of light extraordinarily exact. How did artists achieve this long before photography existed? Part of the answer may lie in an optical device called the camera obscura. The camera obscura, Latin for dark chamber, is beautifully simple. In a darkened room, light passes through a tiny hole in one wall and projects an inverted image of the scene outside onto the opposite wall. The principle was known to ancient scholars, but during the Renaissance, artists began to explore it as a practical tool. Picture a painter inside such a room. The projected image lets the artist trace the outlines of objects, capturing their exact proportions and the precise angles of perspective. Some scholars argue this helped explain the remarkable optical accuracy in works by certain Dutch and Italian masters. Now, this idea remains debated. We have little direct documentation of artists confessing to using the device, and skilled painters certainly mastered perspective through mathematics and observation as well. So we shouldn't assume every realistic painting was traced. But the camera obscura matters for a deeper reason. It marks a moment when art and optical science began to converge, when painters started thinking about vision itself, about how light and the eye actually construct an image. That curiosity about the mechanics of seeing would eventually lead, centuries later, to the invention of the photographic camera. In that sense, the dark chamber was a quiet ancestor of the photograph.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the photographic camera was finally invented in modern times
options.B: Why Dutch and Italian masters preferred painting indoor scenes
options.C: How ancient scholars first described the principles of optics
options.D: How the camera obscura may have shaped Renaissance painting

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 概括主旨：暗箱如何可能影响文艺复兴绘画。A 谈摄影机的发明，那只是结尾的延伸，属 wrong_topic。B 说大师偏好室内场景，讲座未如此说，是 surface_word_trap。C 谈古代学者描述光学，只是一个背景细节，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how does a camera obscura work?

options.A: Light enters a dark room through a small hole, projecting an inverted image
options.B: A curved mirror enlarges a scene and reflects it onto a canvas
options.C: A chemical coating on a wall records the colors of bright light
options.D: Lenses gather light and store the image for later viewing

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与讲座一致：光通过小孔进入暗室，投射出倒置影像。B 说曲面镜放大场景，讲座未提镜子，是 wrong_detail。C 说墙上化学涂层记录颜色，那是后来的摄影，属 topic_adjacent。D 说镜头储存影像供日后查看，暗箱并不储存影像，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out that there is little documentation of artists using the device?

options.A: To describe how the camera obscura projects an inverted image
options.B: To caution that the device's role in painting remains uncertain
options.C: To prove that no Renaissance artist ever used the camera obscura
options.D: To argue that mathematics was the only true source of perspective

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 说明该句目的：提醒暗箱在绘画中的作用仍不确定。A 只是描述暗箱如何成像，与该句意图无关，属 wrong_purpose。C 把缺乏记录夸大为绝对没人用过，是教授未下的过度推断，属 over_inference。D 说数学是透视唯一来源，教授只说画家也通过数学和观察掌握透视，并非唯一，属 wrong_purpose/over。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the camera obscura's broader significance?

options.A: It made traditional training in perspective completely unnecessary
options.B: It proves that all realistic Renaissance paintings were traced
options.C: It reflects an emerging link between art and the science of vision
options.D: It was used mainly by sculptors rather than by painters

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与教授的深层观点一致：暗箱标志着艺术与视觉科学开始交汇。A 说它使透视训练完全不必要，但教授说画家仍靠数学与观察，属 over_generalization。B 说所有写实画都是描摹的，教授明确警告不要这样假设，属 reversed_logic。D 说主要被雕塑家使用，讲座未支持，属 topic_adjacent。
